title: Stories Beneath the Floorboards
synopsis: When an adult discovers old letters in their grandmother's shed, they remember the day they met Cobble, a stubborn gnome living in the secret tunnels below. With sunset approaching and a promise to keep, unlikely friendship blooms in the darkness beneath the garden.

chapters.title: The Letters in the Dust
chapters.content:
Forty years later, I still remember the smell of damp earth and forgotten secrets.

I'm cleaning out Grandmother's old garden shed—the one that's been locked since she passed—when I find them. Three letters tied with string, hidden beneath a loose floorboard that creaks like it's been keeping secrets too long. The paper is yellow and soft, covered in handwriting I don't recognize. But what makes my breath catch is the signature at the bottom of each one: "Your friend always, Cobble."

Cobble. I haven't thought that name in decades.

I sink onto an overturned crate, and suddenly I'm eight years old again, standing in this very shed on the day everything changed. The day the sun was setting too fast, and I had a promise to keep to someone the grown-ups would never believe existed.

Let me tell you about that day. Let me tell you about the tunnels beneath this shed, and the stubborn little gnome who taught me that friendship doesn't care about species or size or whether anyone else thinks you're real.

illustrations.description: An adult kneeling in a dusty garden shed, holding yellowed letters, with golden afternoon light streaming through a small window. Old garden tools and cobwebs fill the corners. Enchanted forest, glowing mushrooms, fairy lights, mystical creatures, rich greens and purples, golden sparkles, Studio Ghibli inspired, magical atmosphere, detailed foliage.
illustrations.alt: Adult discovering old letters in grandmother's shed

chapters.title: The First Crack
chapters.content:
It was a Tuesday in late October when I first heard the tapping.

I'd been hiding in Grandmother's shed—again. Not because I was in trouble (though I probably was), but because sometimes eight-year-olds need a place where adults can't find them with their questions and their "helpful" suggestions about making friends at the new school.

The shed smelled like old wood and plant food, with that particular mustiness that comes from years of storing secrets. I was sitting cross-legged on the wooden floor, trying to read by the light filtering through the dusty window, when I heard it: tap-tap-tap, tap-tap.

Not like a woodpecker. Not like rain. Like someone very small was knocking on wood from underneath the floor.

"Hello?" I whispered, feeling ridiculous.

The tapping stopped.

Then, so quietly I almost missed it: "Finally. Someone with manners."

I looked around the shed. Empty except for me, some rusty tools, and a growing sense that my day was about to get much more interesting.

illustrations.description: A child sitting cross-legged on wooden floorboards in a dim garden shed, looking down with curiosity as mysterious tapping sounds come from below. Dust motes float in shafts of light from a small window. Enchanted forest, glowing mushrooms, fairy lights, mystical creatures, rich greens and purples, golden sparkles, Studio Ghibli inspired, magical atmosphere, detailed foliage.
illustrations.alt: Child hearing mysterious tapping from beneath the shed floor

chapters.title: Down Below
chapters.content:
"Who's there?" I asked the floorboards, which felt like the most sensible thing to do under the circumstances.

"Name's Cobble," came the voice from below. "And I've got a problem that requires someone your size. Are you the helpful sort, or the sort who screams and runs away?"

I considered this. "I'm the sort who hides in sheds."

"Good enough. There's a loose board near your left foot. Pull it up."

I felt around until I found a board that wiggled. When I lifted it, cool air rushed up, carrying the scent of moss and underground streams. Below was darkness, and two bright black eyes looking up at me.

"Well," said Cobble, "are you coming down or not? The sun's getting lower, and I haven't got all day."

That should have been my first clue about the trouble we were heading into. But when you're eight and someone offers you a secret tunnel under your grandmother's shed, you don't ask questions. You climb down.

The tunnel was bigger than I expected, with smooth dirt walls and a ceiling high enough for me to stand. Cobble was exactly as small as his voice suggested—a tiny gnome with a round stone-grey body, white beard, bright black eyes, and a red pointy cap that bobbed when he walked.

illustrations.description: A child peering down through a hole in wooden floorboards into a mysterious underground tunnel, where a small friendly gnome with a round stone-grey body, white beard, bright black eyes, and a red pointy cap looks up at them. The tunnel walls are smooth earth with tiny glowing mushrooms. Enchanted forest, glowing mushrooms, fairy lights, mystical creatures, rich greens and purples, golden sparkles, Studio Ghibli inspired, magical atmosphere, detailed foliage.
illustrations.alt: Child looking down into tunnel where Cobble the gnome waits

chapters.title: The Problem with Stubborn
chapters.content:
"Right," said Cobble, not bothering with pleasantries. "Here's the situation. See that passage there?" He pointed down a narrow tunnel that curved away into darkness. "Leads to my workshop. Been working on something important for months. Problem is, there's been a cave-in, and the passage is too small for me to clear it, but too narrow for most humans."

"But not too narrow for me?"

"Exactly. You're perfectly medium-sized." He said this like it was a compliment, which was nice. Adults were always telling me I was too something—too quiet, too stubborn, too much in my own head.

"What's in your workshop?"

Cobble's expression grew stubborn, which on a gnome face looked like someone had carved determination into stone. "Something that needs to be finished by sunset. And before you ask, no, I can't tell you what it is. Not yet."

I looked toward the entrance of the tunnel. Even from here, I could see golden afternoon light slanting through the hole in the floor above. "How long until sunset?"

"Two hours. Maybe less."

"And if we don't make it?"

Cobble was quiet for a moment. Then: "Someone very important will think I forgot about them. And I never forget."

There was something in his voice—a gentleness beneath the stubbornness—that made my chest tight. I knew what it felt like to think someone had forgotten about you.

illustrations.description: In an underground tunnel lit by soft glowing mushrooms, a small friendly gnome with a round stone-grey body, white beard, bright black eyes, and a red pointy cap points toward a narrow passage blocked with fallen earth and stones. A child stands beside him, both figures dwarfed by the mysterious tunnel system. Enchanted forest, glowing mushrooms, fairy lights, mystical creatures, rich greens and purples, golden sparkles, Studio Ghibli inspired, magical atmosphere, detailed foliage.
illustrations.alt: Cobble showing the child the blocked tunnel passage

chapters.title: Digging Deep
chapters.content:
The work was harder than it looked.

Cobble had made it sound simple: crawl through the passage, clear away the loose dirt and rocks, open the way to his workshop. What he hadn't mentioned was that the tunnel was barely wider than my shoulders, or that the fallen earth was packed tight as concrete, or that every few minutes he'd call out, "How's it going in there?" in the tone of someone trying very hard not to sound worried.

"Fine," I called back, spitting dirt. This was a lie. My knees were scraped, my clothes were filthy, and I was starting to wonder if I was crazy for trusting a gnome I'd met five minutes ago.

But then I thought about his voice when he'd said "I never forget," and I kept digging.

Slowly, handfuls at a time, I cleared a path. The tunnel smelled like earthworms and old secrets. My fingers found stones, roots, and once something that might have been a very old coin. Finally, after what felt like hours, I saw light ahead—not sunlight, but a soft golden glow like candlelight.

"I can see your workshop!" I called back.

"About time," came Cobble's voice, but I could hear relief in it. "What do you see?"

I squeezed through the last bit of tunnel and emerged into the most amazing room I'd ever seen.

illustrations.description: A child crawling through a narrow earthen tunnel, dirt falling around them, with golden light glowing at the end of the passage. The tunnel walls are packed earth with tiny roots poking through, and the child is reaching forward determinedly. Enchanted forest, glowing mushrooms, fairy lights, mystical creatures, rich greens and purples, golden sparkles, Studio Ghibli inspired, magical atmosphere, detailed foliage.
illustrations.alt: Child crawling through the narrow blocked tunnel toward golden light

chapters.title: The Workshop of Wonders
chapters.content:
Cobble's workshop was carved into the earth like a pocket of magic beneath the ordinary world.

Candles in glass jars hung from the ceiling, casting dancing shadows on walls lined with tiny shelves. The shelves held things I couldn't quite identify—bottles of colored sand, bundles of silver wire, seeds that gleamed like jewels. In the center of the room stood a worktable covered with what looked like the pieces of a puzzle made from moonlight and memory.

But it wasn't the magical supplies that made me catch my breath. It was the photographs.

Hundreds of them, pinned to every available surface. Black and white pictures of people I didn't recognize, but whose faces told stories of long journeys and new beginnings. Women in elegant dresses standing beside ships. Families with suitcases, looking hopefully toward something beyond the camera's view. And in the center of it all, one photo larger than the rest: a young woman with kind eyes and a familiar smile.

"That's your grandmother," Cobble said, appearing beside me. For someone so small, he moved remarkably quietly. "When she first came to this country. Scared, stubborn, and braver than she knew."

I stared at the photo. I'd never seen Grandmother look so young, so uncertain. "You knew her?"

"Know her," Cobble corrected. "Present tense. She visits sometimes, when the world gets too heavy. We have tea and remember."

Now I understood the urgency in his voice. "Today's her birthday," I whispered.

"Her seventieth. And I always make something special for the important ones."

illustrations.description: An magical underground workshop carved into earth walls, with candles in glass jars hanging from the ceiling, tiny shelves filled with bottles and magical supplies, and hundreds of old photographs pinned to the walls. In the center, a small friendly gnome with a round stone-grey body, white beard, bright black eyes, and a red pointy cap works at a table covered with shimmering materials. A child stands amazed beside him. Enchanted forest, glowing mushrooms, fairy lights, mystical creatures, rich greens and purples, golden sparkles, Studio Ghibli inspired, magical atmosphere, detailed foliage.
illustrations.alt: Cobble's magical workshop filled with candles, photos, and mysterious supplies

chapters.title: Stories in Silver and Light
chapters.content:
"What are you making?" I asked, moving closer to the worktable.

Cobble's stubborn expression softened into something almost shy. "A memory keeper. For all the stories that matter."

He showed me how it worked—silver wire twisted into delicate spirals, each one holding a tiny glass bead filled with what looked like captured starlight. "Each bead holds a story," he explained. "The ship that brought her here. The first job she found. The day she met your grandfather. The morning you were born."

I watched, fascinated, as his small fingers worked with the precision of someone who'd been crafting magic for centuries. "How do you know all these stories?"

"She tells me. And I remember, because that's what friends do for each other."

We worked together as the afternoon light faded above us. I held wire while Cobble twisted it into shape. I sorted beads while he whispered stories into each one—stories of courage and kindness, of making a home in a place that didn't always feel welcoming, of raising children who would grow up to be braver than their fears.

"There," Cobble said finally, holding up the finished piece. It was a bracelet made of silver wire and starlight, beautiful enough to make my throat tight. "Perfect."

Then we heard it—footsteps above, and Grandmother's voice calling my name.

illustrations.description: A child and a small friendly gnome with a round stone-grey body, white beard, bright black eyes, and a red pointy cap working together at a magical workbench, crafting a delicate silver bracelet with glowing glass beads. Candles provide warm light as they concentrate on the intricate work, surrounded by photos and magical supplies. Enchanted forest, glowing mushrooms, fairy lights, mystical creatures, rich greens and purples, golden sparkles, Studio Ghibli inspired, magical atmosphere, detailed foliage.
illustrations.alt: Child and Cobble working together to create the magical memory bracelet

chapters.title: Sunset Promises
chapters.content:
"She's early," Cobble said, but he was smiling.

"What do I tell her about where I've been?"

"The truth, if you want. Or nothing at all. Adults see what they're ready to see." He wrapped the bracelet carefully in a piece of soft cloth. "But first, will you do me one more favor?"

I nodded.

"Give this to her. Tell her it's from an old friend who never forgets."

I took the small package, warm in my hands. "Will I see you again?"

Cobble's bright black eyes twinkled. "Maybe. If you ever need someone to remember stories with. Or if you ever want to help with another impossible deadline."

I climbed back through the tunnel—much easier now that it was clear—and replaced the loose floorboard just as Grandmother opened the shed door.

"There you are," she said, but not like she was angry. More like she was relieved to find me exactly where I was supposed to be. "What have you been up to?"

I held out the package. "Someone asked me to give this to you. For your birthday."

Grandmother unwrapped it slowly, and when she saw the bracelet, her eyes went wide. Then soft. Then bright with tears that weren't sad.

"Oh," she whispered, fastening it around her wrist where it caught the last light of the setting sun. "Oh, my dear friend."

She looked at me then, really looked, and I saw something in her face that told me she understood exactly who had sent it.

"Thank you," she said simply. "For helping with something important."

That night, I wrote three letters and slipped them through the floorboard crack. I never got a response, but somehow, I knew Cobble had received them.

Now, forty years later, I hold his letters in return, and I remember what it feels like to keep a promise just as the sun sets. Some friendships, I've learned, don't need regular maintenance. They just need someone who never forgets.

In the distance, I hear Grandmother's voice, still calling my name. Still trusting that I'll be exactly where I'm supposed to be, helping with something important.

Some things, it turns out, never change. And maybe that's exactly how it should be.

The magical bracelet catches and reflects the warm light streaming through the window. questions.question: Why does the narrator first go down into the tunnel?
choices: Cobble needs help clearing a cave-in
questions.explanation: Cobble explains that there's been a cave-in blocking his workshop, and he needs someone the right size to help clear it.

questions.question: What makes the situation urgent for Cobble?
choices: He must finish something by sunset
questions.explanation: Cobble explains that something important needs to be finished by sunset, and someone important will think he forgot about them if it's not done.

questions.question: What is special about Grandmother's birthday?
questions.explanation: The narrator mentions that it's Grandmother's seventieth birthday, which is why Cobble is making something special.

questions.question: What does Cobble create for Grandmother?
questions.explanation: Cobble creates a memory keeper bracelet made of silver wire and glass beads, with each bead containing a story from Grandmother's life.

questions.question: How does Grandmother react when she receives the gift?
choices: She's moved and understands who sent it
She thinks it's from the store
questions.explanation: When Grandmother sees the bracelet, her eyes show understanding, and she says 'Oh, my dear friend,' showing she knows exactly who sent it.

questions.question: What does the narrator discover about their grandmother from the photos?
choices: She was born in this country
She was an immigrant who came by ship
questions.explanation: The workshop contains photos of immigrants, including one of Grandmother when she first came to the country, looking scared but brave.

questions.question: What does the story suggest about friendship?
choices: Friends must see each other daily
True friendship doesn't need regular maintenance
Friends should be the same age
questions.explanation: The narrator concludes that some friendships 'don't need regular maintenance. They just need someone who never forgets,' showing friendship can endure across time and differences.
